---
title: "Deliverable Report: The Geography of Disparity in Diabetes Clinical Trial Access"
author: "Chen Zhang"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    self-contained: true
    theme: cosmo
---

# Github Repo
https://github.com/shifosss/JSC370-Midterm-Proj

# Introduction

## Background and Motivation
Diabetes mellitus represents a major public health crisis in the United States, exhibiting profound geographic and socioeconomic disparities (Barker et al., 2011). Socioeconomic status, including factors such as poverty, education, and health insurance, plays a critical role in the prevalence and management of the disease (Hill-Briggs et al., 2021). As the medical industry continues to develop innovative treatments for Type 2 diabetes, a paradox remains: the opportunities to participate in clinical trials are not distributed equitably across the national geography. Clinical trial locations are frequently driven by institutional infrastructure and socioeconomic environments, creating a spatial mismatch that systematically excludes vulnerable, rural, and high-burden populations from research (Tanner et al., 2015; Unger et al., 2019). This lack of access often manifests in the form of physical travel barriers and a lack of local trial availability, directly limiting the generalizability of clinical research (Kirkwood et al., 2024). 

## Research Questions and Hypotheses
This project addresses two related but distinct aims:

1. **Descriptive aim:** To what extent is the geographic distribution of U.S. Type 2 diabetes clinical trial sites aligned with the spatial pattern of disease burden? Where are the largest gaps between trial availability and local need?

2. **Modeling aim:** After adjusting for county-level socioeconomic and demographic characteristics, are clinical trial access measures (local trial density and distance to the nearest trial site) independently associated with county-level diabetes prevalence, or is trial access largely redundant once structural disadvantage is accounted for?

We hypothesize that (1) trial sites are spatially concentrated in socioeconomically advantaged counties with established research infrastructure rather than in the highest-burden areas, and (2) trial-access variables will contribute little independent explanatory power for county diabetes burden once socioeconomic factors are controlled for, consistent with both trial placement and disease burden being downstream of the same structural determinants.

# Methods

## Data Acquisition
The full dataset was assembled exclusively via programmatic API pulls to compile a multi-dimensional, county-level dataset of the United States. We obtained four categories of data, each pinned to a specific reference period:

1. **Clinical trial access** — ClinicalTrials.gov v2 API: all U.S. Type 2 diabetes studies ever registered (no date filter), providing a cumulative snapshot of where trial infrastructure has been placed.
2. **Disease burden** — CDC PLACES: county-level age-adjusted diabetes prevalence and related health measures, retaining the latest available year per county-measure pair (2022 release cycle, with underlying BRFSS data covering 2020–2022).
3. **Socioeconomic context** — American Community Survey (ACS) 5-Year 2022 estimates (pooling survey responses from 2018–2022) for county and state covariates including poverty, income, education, insurance, and demographic composition.
4. **Healthcare infrastructure** — NPI Registry: endocrinologist counts and academic medical center presence by county.

Supplementary contextual variables were drawn from the 2020 Census Decennial (rural population share by state) and a hardcoded lookup of Medicaid expansion status as of January 2024. The 2022 Census Gazetteer and FCC Census API were used for geocoding (see below).

All sources are cross-sectional. Because the ACS 5-Year estimates represent a rolling average centered roughly on 2020, the CDC PLACES burden estimates reflect a similar BRFSS window (2020–2022), and the cumulative trial registry captures long-run placement patterns, temporal misalignment across sources is modest. The unit of analysis — county structural characteristics and trial infrastructure — changes slowly relative to the one- to two-year offsets between data vintages.

## Data Cleaning and Wrangling
The raw nested JSON payloads from ClinicalTrials.gov were flattened into separate trial-level and site-level records. Because the clinical trial data provided city/state locations rather than standard FIPS identifiers, we used the 2022 Census Gazetteer combined with the FCC Census API (reverse geocoding latitude/longitude to FIPS codes) to map trial sites to 5-digit County FIPS codes. To measure geographic isolation, we calculated the straight-line distance (using the Haversine formula and `scipy.spatial.cKDTree`) from each county's population centroid to the nearest clinical trial site. All datasets were merged on the County FIPS code. Missing values for core modeling features were handled via complete-case analysis. Highly skewed variables (total population, trial distance, and trial density) were log-transformed (`log1p` or `log`) to stabilize variance.

## Analytical Tools and Modeling
EDA and feature engineering used pandas and numpy. Visualizations were produced with seaborn and matplotlib. For the modeling aim, the response variable is **county-level age-adjusted diabetes prevalence** (`places_diabetes` from CDC PLACES). The 13 predictors fall into two groups: *trial-access variables* (log trial density, log distance to nearest site) and *socioeconomic/demographic covariates* (poverty rate, median household income, Gini index, uninsured rate, unemployment rate, bachelor's degree attainment, vehicle access, internet access, % non-Hispanic Black, % Hispanic, and log population).

The modeling strategy proceeds in two stages:

**Primary inferential model.** An OLS linear regression estimates the association between each predictor and county diabetes prevalence, yielding standardized coefficients, 95% confidence intervals, and p-values. An Elastic Net variant (scikit-learn `ElasticNetCV`, 5-fold CV over five `l1_ratio` values and 100 `alpha` values, predictors standardized) is also fit to verify that coefficient estimates are stable under regularization when predictors are correlated. The inferential conclusions rest on the OLS/Elastic Net coefficients: whether trial-access variables are statistically associated with diabetes burden after adjustment for socioeconomic structure.

**Secondary ML models.** Random Forest (500 trees, permutation importance) and XGBoost (gradient-boosted trees) are fit on the same predictor set to check whether non-linear relationships or interactions meaningfully improve explanatory power beyond the linear model. SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values from the XGBoost model provide a supplementary view of relative feature importance, but SHAP measures predictive contribution, not statistical association, and is therefore treated as a secondary check rather than the primary evidence.

All models were evaluated under the same 5-fold cross-validation scheme (shuffled, `random_state=42`). Performance was compared on cross-validated R², RMSE, and MAE.

# Preliminary Results
The data pipeline aggregated 3,646 U.S. Type 2 diabetes studies and 47,118 site records. The descriptive results presented below address Aim 1 at the state level, where every state has trial activity, and then extend to the county level to examine spatial access gaps. Because 73.4% of U.S. counties host no diabetes trial site at all, state-level summaries serve as the primary descriptive lens; county-level results supplement them by quantifying the within-state access variation that state aggregates mask.

## State-Level Burden and Trial Density Do Not Line Up Cleanly

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
![State-level diabetes age-adjusted prevalence (%).](option-c-trial-access/discover/results/map_diabetes_prevalence_tile.png){width=100%}
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
![State-level diabetes trial-site density (trials per 100k population).](option-c-trial-access/discover/results/map_trial_density_tile.png){width=100%}
:::
:::

Figure 1 shows the central descriptive mismatch. States with heavier diabetes burden are not automatically the states with denser trial availability. The diabetes belt remains visible in the South and Appalachia, but the trial-density map is more fragmented and includes several smaller, infrastructure-rich states that do not rank among the highest-burden areas. This visual divergence motivates a more explicit comparison between observed and expected trial density.

To formalize that mismatch, I computed a state-level coverage residual: observed trial density minus the trial density expected for a state's diabetes-burden decile. Negative values indicate under-coverage relative to peer states with similar burden, while positive values indicate over-coverage.

![Coverage residual outliers by state. Each bar represents a state's coverage residual (observed trial density minus the mean density for its diabetes-burden decile). Bars are colored by the percentage of that state's trials that are industry-sponsored (i.e., the lead sponsor is a pharmaceutical or device company rather than an academic institution, NIH, or other non-industry entity). Darker or more saturated bars indicate a higher industry share.](option-c-trial-access/discover/results/coverage_residual_outlier_bars.png){width=100%}

The residual analysis sharpens the map comparison. Several high-burden or moderately high-burden states appear under-covered relative to peers in the same burden decile, while some Mountain Plains and small-jurisdiction states appear over-covered. Sponsor composition is also concentrated: nationally, industry-sponsored trial density is 5.39 trials per 100k population versus 0.78 for non-industry trials, so industry density is about 6.9 times higher. This pattern is consistent with the possibility that site placement is influenced by sponsor logistics and institutional presence, though the residual analysis alone cannot confirm that mechanism.

**Table 1. Five most under-covered states relative to diabetes burden**

| State | Trial count | Site count | Trials per 100k | Coverage residual | Industry share (%) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| WY | 1 | 1 | 0.17 | -8.58 | 0.0 |
| AK | 8 | 9 | 1.09 | -7.66 | 87.5 |
| NH | 98 | 103 | 7.10 | -5.11 | 96.9 |
| WI | 221 | 285 | 3.76 | -4.50 | 88.2 |
| CO | 458 | 685 | 7.94 | -4.28 | 80.8 |

**Table 2. Five most over-covered states relative to diabetes burden**

| State | Trial count | Site count | Trials per 100k | Coverage residual | Industry share (%) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| ID | 301 | 376 | 16.23 | 7.48 | 99.7 |
| ND | 119 | 132 | 15.32 | 6.57 | 99.2 |
| DC | 125 | 136 | 18.64 | 6.43 | 70.4 |
| NE | 283 | 404 | 14.45 | 6.19 | 92.6 |
| KS | 351 | 475 | 11.96 | 4.92 | 94.6 |

Together, Figure 1, Figure 2, and Tables 1–2 suggest that trial availability is not uniformly low or high across states; rather, there is substantial variation in how well trial density aligns with burden. Some states appear under-supplied relative to their burden decile peers, while others receive disproportionately dense coverage. These descriptive patterns motivate further investigation but do not, on their own, identify the mechanisms behind the mismatch.

## County-Level Access Gaps Are Large

While state-level summaries provide a tractable overview, they can mask substantial within-state variation. The county file retains 3,221 counties. Only 856 counties (26.6%) host at least one diabetes trial site, while 2,365 counties (73.4%) have none. This sparsity is an important caveat: county-level trial density is zero-inflated, so county-level modeling (Aim 2) relies heavily on the distance-to-nearest-site measure rather than local trial counts to capture access variation.

**Table 3. County-level access summary**

| Metric | Value |
|---|---:|
| Counties in final county file | 3,221 |
| Counties with at least one trial site | 856 (26.6%) |
| Counties without a trial site | 2,365 (73.4%) |
| Median nearest-site distance among no-site counties | 58.4 km |
| Share of no-site counties more than 50 km away | 59.7% |
| Share of no-site counties more than 100 km away | 24.0% |
| Share of no-site counties more than 200 km away | 8.2% |

![Distance to the nearest diabetes trial site for counties without a local site.](option-c-trial-access/discover/results/county_distance_histogram.png){width=100%}

This figure shows that the access problem is not marginal. For counties without a site, travel distance is often substantial rather than trivial. That pattern matters for the research question because a state can look moderately well-covered in aggregate while still leaving most of its counties without feasible local trial access.

## Endocrinologist Density and Trial Density Co-Occur

Among counties that host at least one trial site, those with higher endocrinologist density also tend to have higher trial density (Figure 4). This bivariate association is descriptive and does not establish a causal link; both variables may simply reflect the presence of academic medical centers or large health systems.

![Bivariate relationship between county endocrinologist density and county trial density. Each point represents a county with at least one trial site. The association is positive but noisy, and the scatter includes substantial variability.](option-c-trial-access/discover/results/county_endo_vs_trials.png){width=85%}

This co-occurrence is consistent with the hypothesis that trial placement tracks institutional infrastructure, but the bivariate scatter alone cannot distinguish infrastructure-driven placement from other explanations (e.g., population size driving both). The multivariable models in the final analysis (Aim 2) are designed to disentangle these overlapping factors.

# Summary
The descriptive results are consistent with the project hypothesis but do not yet confirm it. At the state level, trial density does not align smoothly with diabetes burden, and the coverage-residual analysis reveals systematic variation across burden deciles. At the county level, the majority of counties (73.4%) host no trial site, and travel distances for residents of those counties are often substantial. Endocrinologist density and trial density co-occur, but this bivariate pattern cannot isolate the role of infrastructure from confounding factors such as population size.

These findings are exploratory and descriptive. They identify suggestive patterns but cannot determine whether trial-access variables retain an independent association with county-level diabetes prevalence (the response variable for Aim 2) once socioeconomic, demographic, and infrastructure factors are considered jointly.

For the final deliverable, the plan is:

1. **Primary inferential model:** Fit an OLS linear regression of county-level age-adjusted diabetes prevalence on trial-access variables (log trial density, log distance to nearest site) and socioeconomic/demographic covariates. Report standardized coefficients, confidence intervals, and p-values to assess whether trial-access variables are independently associated with diabetes burden after adjustment.
2. **Secondary ML models:** Fit Random Forest and XGBoost models on the same predictor set to check whether non-linear relationships or interactions substantially improve explanatory power beyond the linear model. Use SHAP values from the best-performing ML model as a supplementary lens on relative feature importance, recognizing that SHAP measures predictive importance rather than statistical association.
3. **Interactive visualizations:** Provide interactive maps and exploratory tools that allow users to examine the spatial relationships between burden, access, and infrastructure at both the state and county levels.

# References

Barker, L. E., Kirtland, K. A., Gregg, E. W., Geiss, L. S., & Thompson, T. J. (2011). Geographic distribution of diagnosed diabetes in the U.S.: A diabetes belt. American Journal of Preventive Medicine, 40(4), 434–439. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.amepre.2010.12.019

Hill-Briggs, F., Adler, N. E., Berkowitz, S. A., Chin, M. H., Gary-Webb, T. L., Navas-Acien, A., Thornton, P. L., & Haire-Joshu, D. (2021). Social determinants of health and diabetes: A scientific review. Diabetes Care, 44(1), 258–279. https://doi.org/10.2337/dci20-0053

Kirkwood, M. K., Schenkel, C., Hinshaw, D. C., Bruinooge, S. S., Waterhouse, D. M., Peppercorn, J. M., Subbiah, I. M., & Levit, L. A. (2024). State of geographic access to cancer treatment trials in the United States: Are studies located where patients live? JCO Oncology Practice, 20(3), 427–437.

Tanner, A., Kim, S. H., Friedman, D. B., Foster, C., & Bergeron, C. D. (2015). Barriers to medical research participation as perceived by clinical trial investigators: Communicating with rural and African American communities. Journal of Health Communication, 20(1), 88–96. https://doi.org/10.1080/10810730.2014.908985

Unger, J. M., Vaidya, R., Hershman, D. L., Minasian, L. M., & Fleury, M. E. (2019). Systematic review and meta-analysis of the magnitude of structural, clinical, and physician and patient barriers to cancer clinical trial participation. Journal of the National Cancer Institute, 111(3), 245–255.
